Row1.Rationale: In KRAS G12C‑mutant lung cancer, sotorasib traps KRAS in the GDP‑bound state, reducing ERK phosphorylation and downstream signaling in sensitive cells; in resistant models, PAK‑mediated MEK and CRAF phosphorylation and broader MAPK pathway reactivation restore proliferation despite continued KRAS inhibition, and clinical autopsy data show MAPK reactivation in most post‑sotorasib NSCLC lesions.

Row2.Rationale: Sotorasib reduces AKT phosphorylation in sensitive KRAS G12C lung and pancreatic lines, whereas resistant cells display constitutively active PI3K/AKT; enforced PI3K activation or PTEN loss induces resistance, and PI3K inhibition (alpelisib) synergizes with sotorasib, while clinical resistant NSCLC tissue shows AKT/mTOR pathway activation after treatment.

Row3.Rationale: Sotorasib‑resistant KRAS G12C cells show enhanced cell–matrix adhesion with increased stress fibers and focal adhesions, accompanied by Src activation and PAK phosphorylation; activated PAK phosphorylates MEK (S298) and CRAF (S338), re‑engaging MAPK signaling, and PAK inhibitors (FRAX597/FRAX486) restore sotorasib sensitivity in KRAS G12C‑mutant models.

Row4.Rationale: Resistant cells exhibit upregulated PAK phosphorylation independent of Cdc42/Rac1 activation; PAK directly activates MEK and CRAF and participates in a mutual positive regulatory loop with PI3K, maintaining both MAPK and PI3K signaling under sotorasib, and dual PAK plus KRAS G12C inhibition synergistically reduces viability of KRAS G12C‑mutant cancer cells.

Row5.Rationale: Functional studies demonstrate that PI3K and PAK pathways reinforce each other in sotorasib‑resistant cells, such that PI3K activation enhances PAK signaling and vice versa, sustaining ERK and AKT activity; disruption of this loop with PI3K or PAK inhibitors re‑sensitizes KRAS G12C‑mutant cells to sotorasib, highlighting it as a core adaptive resistance network.

Row6.Rationale: Autopsy‑based profiling of a KRAS G12C NSCLC patient progressing on sotorasib revealed activation of YAP signaling among the dominant resistance programs; prior preclinical work cited in this analysis links YAP to EMT and bypass survival signaling, suggesting that YAP‑driven transcription supports MAPK/PI3K reactivation and loss of KRAS oncogene addiction under KRAS G12C inhibition.

Row7.Rationale: Clinical RNA‑seq of pre‑ and post‑sotorasib NSCLC lesions shows enrichment of EMT hallmark gene sets in resistant tumors, and review of KRAS G12C inhibitor data indicates that EMT induction and TGFβ‑EMT signatures correlate with reduced dependence on KRAS and attenuated sensitivity to sotorasib, with mesenchymal states favoring alternative RTK and YAP‑driven signaling.

Row8.Rationale: Gene‑set analysis of sotorasib‑resistant NSCLC metastases demonstrates upregulation of hedgehog, NOTCH, and WNT/β‑catenin signaling, consistent with broad developmental pathway re‑wiring; these pathways can promote stem‑like phenotypes, survival, and immune evasion, providing parallel proliferative inputs that bypass KRAS blockade.

Row9.Rationale: In the same clinical resistance case, angiogenesis and coagulation hallmark pathways were upregulated post‑sotorasib, indicating remodeling of the tumor vasculature and microenvironment; such changes can support tumor growth, drug delivery heterogeneity, and a pro‑thrombotic, pro‑survival niche that facilitates persistence despite KRAS inhibition.

Row10.Rationale: In KRAS G12C lung cancer models with acquired sotorasib resistance, PD‑L1 upregulation drives expansion and recruitment of myeloid‑derived suppressor cells via JAK2/STAT3/IL‑6 signaling, reducing CD8⁺ T‑cell infiltration and apoptosis; pathway enrichment analyses in resistant tumors similarly identify JAK2/STAT3 activation, and targeting this axis reverses the immunosuppressive microenvironment.

Row11.Rationale: Prolonged AMG‑510 exposure induces PD‑L1 upregulation on KRAS G12C tumor cells and increases PD‑1 expression on CD8⁺ T cells, creating an immunosuppressive microenvironment with reduced cytotoxic T‑cell infiltration; PD‑L1 blockade or PD‑1–directed combinations restore antitumor immunity and re‑sensitize resistant tumors to sotorasib, and preclinical data show synergy of KRAS G12C inhibition with anti‑PD‑1 in T‑cell–infiltrated lung tumors.

Row12.Rationale: In KRAS G12C‑mutant lung cancer cell lines sensitive to sotorasib, KRAS inhibition upregulates CD47 and CD24 on tumor cells via FOXA1‑dependent transcriptional activation, providing anti‑phagocytic signals that protect cells from macrophage‑mediated clearance; combined targeting of KRAS, CD47, and adaptive immune checkpoints overcomes intrinsic resistance to KRAS G12C inhibitor plus PD‑(L)1 therapy in preclinical models.
